ccna-34-standard-acl./title: Standard ACL and Source IPv4 Filtering Placement
ccna-34-standard-acl./summary: Understand top-down processing, wildcard masks, implicit deny, and why a standard ACL should be placed near the destination.
ccna-34-standard-acl./opening/0: BlueRetail is expanding its retail network, and only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. If a Junior Network Engineer looks at only one command or one diagram icon, it is easy to make a change in the wrong place. Lesson 34 begins with this incident to answer a practical question: How do a standard ACL and source IPv4 filtering placement affect the data path, and how can the conclusion be proven with output?
ccna-34-standard-acl./opening/1: Understand top-down processing, wildcard masks, implicit deny, and why a standard ACL should be placed near the destination. The goal is not to memorize every table and acronym. You need to explain the data journey from the moment it leaves the application, passes through the network interface card, switch, or router, and receives a response. Every decision must be tied to an observable header field, state table, policy, or physical limitation.
ccna-34-standard-acl./opening/2: The Jeremy’s IT Lab PDF provides the foundational sequence and examples on pages 226–231; the exam scope is aligned with objective 5.6 of Cisco CCNA v1.1. The content is reinterpreted using the BlueRetail topology and new data, without copying figures or text verbatim from the source material.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/0/title: A Mental Model Before Learning Commands
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of a network as a chain of evidence-based handoffs. The application creates data, the operating system encapsulates it, the access device places it on the LAN, the router selects a path between networks, and the destination service responds. The Standard ACL lesson focuses on one or more handoff points in that chain. Unless you identify the layer at which the data currently resides and the device authorized to modify it, reading the configuration quickly becomes guesswork.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/0/paragraphs/1: In the scenario where only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router but the current ACL also blocks internal applications, begin by drawing the source, destination, default gateway, and intermediate devices. Next to each hop, note which address or identifier is used, which table is consulted, and what conditions cause the traffic to stop. This model is small enough to draw on paper but precise enough to explain real output.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/0/paragraphs/2: One sign that you understand lesson 34 is that you can predict the state of source-only matching before running a command. If the prediction differs from the output, do not change the configuration immediately; find the incorrect assumption. The predict-observe-explain process helps distinguish configuration errors, physical faults, protocol failures, and errors caused by examining the wrong device.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/1/title: Four Concepts That Must Be Connected
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Source-only matching is the first building block of the Standard ACL lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. Connecting source-only matching with top-down processing allows learners to see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Top-down processing is the second building block of the Standard ACL lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. Connecting top-down processing with the wildcard mask allows learners to see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The wildcard mask is the third building block of the Standard ACL lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. Connecting the wildcard mask with implicit deny allows learners to see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/3: Implicit deny is the fourth building block of the Standard ACL lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. Connecting implicit deny with source-only matching allows learners to see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/2/title: In What Order Does a Device Process Data?
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Track source-only matching across three points in lesson 34. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with the existing state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 1, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of choosing the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Track top-down processing across three points in lesson 34. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with the existing state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 2, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of choosing the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Track the wildcard mask across three points in lesson 34. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with the existing state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 3, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of choosing the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Track implicit deny across three points in lesson 34. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with the existing state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 4, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of choosing the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/3/title: Reading Configuration and CLI Output Without Guessing
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/3/paragraphs/0: The show access-lists 10 command is only an observation window for the Standard ACL lesson; by itself, it does not prove that the entire network is operating correctly. Before reading the output, write down the question that must be answered, such as whether the state exists, which interface is involved, which value has priority, and when the output was collected. Then mark each line that supports or disproves the hypothesis.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/3/paragraphs/1: The three sample lines for the Standard ACL are arranged in this order: identify the object, read the state, and verify the result. If a line is missing, determine whether it should actually appear. Do not treat absence as an error until you understand the conditions that create the entry, its timer, and the command’s display scope.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When the show access-lists 10 output differs from expectations, return to the control plane, data plane, and management plane. The configuration may exist in running-config even though the protocol has not converged; the protocol may be up while a policy still blocks the packet; or the packet may follow the correct path while the management tool reads the wrong VRF or context. Separating these three evidence layers helps narrow the problem quickly in lesson 34.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/4/title: Applying the Knowledge to the BlueRetail Network
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/4/paragraphs/0: At BlueRetail, headquarters has two access switches, a pair of distribution devices, an edge router, a firewall, a WLC, service servers, and branch connectivity. Only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. Learners must first determine the scope of impact: one host, one VLAN, one subnet, one uplink, or the entire site. The clearer the scope, the fewer hypotheses must be tested.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The next step is to select observation points on both sides of the boundary. For a Standard ACL, collect one piece of evidence near the source and another near the decision point. If the two sides are inconsistent, inspect the encapsulation, addresses, timers, policy, or interface state between them; do not immediately restart the device.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Finally, for a Standard ACL, document the conclusion using this structure: observed facts, applicable protocol rule, effect on traffic, and the lowest-risk action. A conclusion that others can verify is better than saying, “the configuration looks correct.” This is also how lesson 34 knowledge becomes practical operational skill.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/5/title: How to Reason Through CCNA Answer Choices
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Objective 5.6 for the Standard ACL lesson indicates the required level through verbs such as explain, compare, configure, verify, or interpret. If the question asks only to describe, do not infer an out-of-scope configuration. If it asks you to verify, the answer must produce evidence rather than merely repeat the configuration. The objective’s verb determines the required depth of study.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/5/paragraphs/1: When lesson 34 provides several partially correct answers, first eliminate those that use the wrong layer, traffic direction, or processing order. Then use source-only matching details such as the prefix, metric, port role, adjacency, or policy placement to choose the answer. Mnemonics are useful only after the processing model is correct.
ccna-34-standard-acl./sections/5/paragraphs/2: The Standard ACL lesson is within the official scope; learners must be able to explain both correct behavior and signs of incorrect behavior. Every answer should end with verifiable output or an observable result.
ccna-34-standard-acl./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram showing the relationships among Source-only matching, Top-down processing, Wildcard mask, and Implicit deny in the BlueRetail network.
ccna-34-standard-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/context: Condensed output for the scenario where only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router but the current ACL also blocks internal applications.
ccna-34-standard-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/0: The first line identifies the object or scope being examined in the Standard ACL lesson.
ccna-34-standard-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/1: The second line shows the state calculated by the device, not merely the configuration intent.
ccna-34-standard-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/2: The third line provides evidence linking the state to its effect on BlueRetail traffic.
ccna-34-standard-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/3: If the actual output differs from the three sample lines, check the timing, context, interface, and entry creation conditions before changing the configuration.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/0/useWhen: Use the source-only matching perspective when the evidence relates to step 1 of the processing sequence.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/0/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines source-only matching to explain why only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router but the current ACL also blocks internal applications.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not equate source-only matching with top-down processing; they answer two different questions.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/1/useWhen: Use the top-down processing perspective when the evidence relates to step 2 of the processing sequence.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/1/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines top-down processing to explain why only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router but the current ACL also blocks internal applications.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not equate top-down processing with a wildcard mask; they answer two different questions.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/2/useWhen: Use the wildcard mask perspective when the evidence relates to step 3 of the processing sequence.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/2/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the wildcard mask to explain why only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router but the current ACL also blocks internal applications.
ccna-34-standard-acl./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not equate a wildcard mask with implicit deny; they answer two different questions.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the Source-only matching keyword and immediately choosing the command or answer containing the same keyword.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/0/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to source-only matching before choosing.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/0/reason: In lesson 34, source-only matching is meaningful only when considered alongside top-down processing and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the Top-down processing keyword and immediately choosing the command or answer containing the same keyword.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/1/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to top-down processing before choosing.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/1/reason: In lesson 34, top-down processing is meaningful only when considered alongside the wildcard mask and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the Wildcard mask keyword and immediately choosing the command or answer containing the same keyword.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/2/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the wildcard mask before choosing.
ccna-34-standard-acl./commonMistakes/2/reason: In lesson 34, the wildcard mask is meaningful only when considered alongside implicit deny and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-34-standard-acl./examFocus/0: Review objective 5.6 and identify the required verb correctly.
ccna-34-standard-acl./examFocus/1: Predict the output of show access-lists 10 before viewing the answer.
ccna-34-standard-acl./examFocus/2: Explain the effects of Source-only matching and Top-down processing on the same packet.
ccna-34-standard-acl./examFocus/3: Recognize common signs of errors without making out-of-scope configuration changes.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/context: BlueRetail reports that only the management subnet is allowed to SSH into the router, but the current ACL also blocks internal applications. You are assigned to analyze the available diagram and output before the operations team permits configuration changes.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/learnerRole: You are a Junior Network Engineer responsible for stating a hypothesis, interpreting the evidence, and proposing the next verification step.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/topology: Client → access switch → distribution/router → destination service; the observation focus is Source-only matching → Top-down processing → Wildcard mask → Implicit deny.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/evidence/0: The configuration or state related to Source-only matching has been provided.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/evidence/1: The show access-lists 10 output contains three lines that must be explained in sequence.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/evidence/2: The user has described the symptom but has not proven that Top-down processing is the cause.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/questions/0: Which output line provides the strongest evidence, and what does it prove about Source-only matching?
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/questions/1: Which hypothesis is disproved when Top-down processing is compared with the Wildcard mask?
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/questions/2: What is the lowest-risk next observation step, and what result would confirm the conclusion?
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Redraw the source, destination, Layer 2/Layer 3 boundaries, and traffic direction.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Mark where Source-only matching is created, learned, or configured.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Read each line of show access-lists 10 and separate facts from assumptions.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Write a conclusion that includes the most likely cause, its impact, and the additional evidence required.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/expectedFindings/0: Source-only matching must be explained using observed state, not only running-config.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/expectedFindings/1: Top-down processing and the Wildcard mask occur at different steps and therefore cannot replace each other.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/expectedFindings/2: The next step must narrow the hypothesis related to the Standard ACL without disrupting traffic.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/rubric/0/evidence: Identify the device, interface, VLAN/subnet, or policy being observed.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/rubric/0/importance: If the scope is wrong, the output may be correct while the conclusion remains incorrect.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Explain the output using at least two concepts from the Standard ACL lesson.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/rubric/1/importance: CCNA assesses reasoning ability, not just command recognition.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The next step has an expected output and does not change the running configuration.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/rubric/2/importance: Observing before making changes reduces disruption and avoids obscuring the root cause.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: First, scope the traffic using the topology and identify Source-only matching as the lesson’s primary decision point.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Next, use show access-lists 10 to compare Standard IP access list 10 with 10 permit 10.10.99.0, wildcard bits 0.0.0.255 (42 matches), separating the actual state from the intended configuration.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Finally, inspect the Wildcard mask at the next hop; propose a change only after new output confirms the cause and a post-change verification method is available.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/commonMistakes/0: Changing the configuration immediately after reading the user’s description.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/commonMistakes/1: Quoting one output line without identifying the device or context to which it belongs.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating a cause without identifying the next output that could confirm or disprove it.
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: If traffic flows in the opposite direction, how does the role of Source-only matching change?
ccna-34-standard-acl./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: What additional evidence would you need to explain the Standard ACL lesson to a colleague who has not studied CCNA?

ccna-34-standard-acl./sources/0/label: Jeremy’s IT Lab CCNA Notes, pages 226–231
ccna-34-standard-acl./sources/1/label: Jeremy’s IT Lab — Standard ACL and Source IPv4 Filtering Placement

ccna-35-extended-acl./title: Extended ACL and Filtering by Protocol, Source, Destination, and Port
ccna-35-extended-acl./summary: Design extended ACLs based on traffic flow, application ports, the established keyword, and the principle of placement close to the source.
ccna-35-extended-acl./opening/0: BlueRetail is expanding its retail system, and POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. If a Junior Network Engineer looks at only one command or one icon in the diagram, it is easy to make a change in the wrong place. Lesson 35 begins with this incident to answer a practical question: how do extended ACLs and filtering by protocol, source, destination, and port affect the data path, and how can the conclusion be proven with output?
ccna-35-extended-acl./opening/1: Design extended ACLs based on traffic flow, application ports, the established keyword, and the principle of placement close to the source. The goal is not to memorize every table and abbreviation. You need to be able to recount the data’s journey from the moment it leaves the application, passes through the network interface card, switch, or router, and receives a response. Every decision must be tied to an observable header field, state table, policy, or physical limitation.
ccna-35-extended-acl./opening/2: The Jeremy’s IT Lab PDF provides the foundational sequence and examples on pages 232–239; the exam scope is aligned with objective 5.6 of Cisco CCNA v1.1. The content is reinterpreted using the BlueRetail topology and new data, without copying figures or text verbatim from the source material.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/0/title: A Mental Model Before Learning Commands
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of the network as a chain of handoffs supported by evidence. The application creates data, the operating system encapsulates it, the access device places it on the LAN, the router selects a path between networks, and the destination service responds. This Extended ACL lesson focuses on one or more handoff points in that chain. If you have not identified the layer at which the data currently resides and which device can modify it, reading the configuration will quickly become guesswork.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/0/paragraphs/1: In the scenario where POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver while all other traffic must be blocked, begin by drawing the source, destination, default gateway, and intermediate devices. Then note beside each hop which address or identifier is used, which table is consulted, and which conditions cause traffic to stop. This model is small enough to draw on paper but precise enough to explain actual output.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/0/paragraphs/2: One sign that you understand lesson 35 is that you can predict the state of protocol matching before running a command. If the prediction and output differ, do not change the configuration immediately; find the incorrect assumption. The predict-observe-explain process helps distinguish configuration errors, physical faults, protocol failures, and errors caused by examining the wrong device.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Protocol matching is the first component of the Extended ACL lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. Connecting protocol matching with source/destination fields allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Source/destination fields are the second component of the Extended ACL lesson. Do not learn them as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. Connecting source/destination fields with port operators allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Port operators are the third component of the Extended ACL lesson. Do not learn them as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. Connecting port operators with ACL placement allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/1/paragraphs/3: ACL placement is the fourth component of the Extended ACL lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. Connecting ACL placement with protocol matching allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/2/title: In What Order Does a Device Process Data?
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Track protocol matching at three points in lesson 35. First, the device receives a frame, packet, control information, or relevant configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 1, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Track source/destination fields at three points in lesson 35. First, the device receives a frame, packet, control information, or relevant configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 2, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Track port operators at three points in lesson 35. First, the device receives a frame, packet, control information, or relevant configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 3, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Track ACL placement at three points in lesson 35. First, the device receives a frame, packet, control information, or relevant configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 4, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/3/title: Reading Configuration and CLI Output Without Guessing
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/3/paragraphs/0: The show ip access-lists POS-POLICY command is only an observation window for the Extended ACL lesson; by itself, it does not prove that the entire network is operating correctly. Before reading it, write down the question to be answered, such as whether the state exists, which interface is involved, which value takes precedence, and when the output was captured. Then mark each line that supports or refutes the hypothesis.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/3/paragraphs/1: The three sample Extended ACL lines are arranged in this order: identify the object, read the state, and verify the result. If a line is missing, determine whether it should actually appear. Do not treat absence as an error until you understand the conditions that create the entry, its timer, and the command’s display scope.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When the output of show ip access-lists POS-POLICY differs from expectations, return to the control plane, data plane, and management plane. The configuration may exist in running-config while the protocol has not converged; the protocol may be up while a policy still blocks the packet; or the packet may follow the correct path while the management tool reads the wrong VRF or context. Separating these three layers of evidence helps narrow the issue quickly in lesson 35.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/4/title: Applying the Concepts to the BlueRetail Network
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/4/paragraphs/0: At BlueRetail, headquarters has two access switches, a pair of distribution devices, an edge router, a firewall, a WLC, service servers, and branch connectivity. POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. Learners must first determine the scope of impact: one host, one VLAN, one subnet, one uplink, or the entire site. The clearer the scope, the fewer hypotheses need to be tested.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The next step is to select observation points on both sides of the boundary. For an Extended ACL, collect one piece of evidence near the source and another near the decision point. If the two sides disagree, check the encapsulation, addresses, timers, policy, or interface state between them; do not jump directly to restarting a device.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Finally, for an Extended ACL, write the conclusion using this structure: observed facts, applicable protocol rule, effect on traffic, and the lowest-risk action. A conclusion that others can verify is better than saying, “the configuration looks correct.” This is also how lesson 35 knowledge becomes practical operational skill.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/5/title: How to Select the Correct CCNA Answer
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Objective 5.6 of the Extended ACL lesson indicates the required level with verbs such as explain, compare, configure, verify, or interpret. If the question asks only to describe, do not infer an out-of-scope configuration; if it asks you to verify, the answer must produce evidence rather than merely repeat the configuration. The objective’s verb determines the required depth of study.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/5/paragraphs/1: When lesson 35 presents several partially correct answers, first eliminate answers that use the wrong layer, traffic direction, or processing order. Then use details related to protocol matching, such as the prefix, metric, port role, adjacency, or policy location, to make the selection. Mnemonics are useful only after the processing model is correct.
ccna-35-extended-acl./sections/5/paragraphs/2: The Extended ACL lesson is within the official scope; learners must be able to explain both correct behavior and signs of incorrect behavior. Every answer should end with verifiable output or an observable result.
ccna-35-extended-acl./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram showing the relationships among Protocol matching, Source/destination fields, Port operators, and ACL placement in the BlueRetail network.
ccna-35-extended-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/context: Condensed output for the scenario in which POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked.
ccna-35-extended-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/0: The first line identifies the object or scope being examined in the Extended ACL lesson.
ccna-35-extended-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/1: The second line shows the state calculated by the device, not merely the configuration intent.
ccna-35-extended-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/2: The third line provides evidence linking the state to its effect on BlueRetail traffic.
ccna-35-extended-acl./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/3: If the actual output differs from the three sample lines, check the timing, context, interface, and entry-creation conditions before changing the configuration.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/0/useWhen: Use the protocol-matching perspective when the evidence relates to step 1 in the processing chain.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/0/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines protocol matching to explain why POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver while all other traffic must be blocked.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not equate protocol matching with source/destination fields; they answer two different questions.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/1/useWhen: Use the source/destination-fields perspective when the evidence relates to step 2 in the processing chain.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/1/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines source/destination fields to explain why POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver while all other traffic must be blocked.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not equate source/destination fields with port operators; they answer two different questions.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/2/useWhen: Use the port-operator perspective when the evidence relates to step 3 in the processing chain.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/2/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines port operators to explain why POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver while all other traffic must be blocked.
ccna-35-extended-acl./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not equate port operators with ACL placement; they answer two different questions.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the term Protocol matching and immediately selecting the command or answer that contains the same term.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/0/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to protocol matching before making a selection.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/0/reason: In lesson 35, protocol matching is meaningful only when considered alongside source/destination fields and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the term Source/destination fields and immediately selecting the command or answer that contains the same term.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/1/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to source/destination fields before making a selection.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/1/reason: In lesson 35, source/destination fields are meaningful only when considered alongside port operators and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the term Port operators and immediately selecting the command or answer that contains the same term.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/2/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to port operators before making a selection.
ccna-35-extended-acl./commonMistakes/2/reason: In lesson 35, port operators are meaningful only when considered alongside ACL placement and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-35-extended-acl./examFocus/0: Review objective 5.6 and identify the required verb correctly.
ccna-35-extended-acl./examFocus/1: Predict the output of show ip access-lists POS-POLICY before viewing the answer.
ccna-35-extended-acl./examFocus/2: Explain the effects of Protocol matching and Source/destination fields on the same packet.
ccna-35-extended-acl./examFocus/3: Recognize common signs of an error without making out-of-scope configuration changes.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/context: BlueRetail reports that POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver; all other traffic must be blocked. You are assigned to analyze the available diagram and output before the operations team permits any configuration changes.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/learnerRole: You are the Junior Network Engineer responsible for stating hypotheses, interpreting evidence, and proposing the next verification step.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/topology: Client → access switch → distribution/router → destination service; the observation focus is Protocol matching → Source/destination fields → Port operators → ACL placement.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/evidence/0: The configuration or state related to Protocol matching has been provided.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/evidence/1: The output of show ip access-lists POS-POLICY contains three lines that must be explained in order.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/evidence/2: The user has described the symptoms but has not proven that the cause lies in the Source/destination fields.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/questions/0: Which output line is the strongest evidence, and what does it prove about Protocol matching?
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/questions/1: Which hypothesis is refuted when Source/destination fields are compared with Port operators?
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/questions/2: What is the lowest-risk next observation step, and what result would confirm the conclusion?
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Redraw the source, destination, Layer 2/Layer 3 boundary, and traffic direction.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Mark where Protocol matching is created, learned, or configured.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Read each line of show ip access-lists POS-POLICY and separate facts from assumptions.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Write a conclusion that includes the most likely cause, its impact, and the additional evidence required.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/expectedFindings/0: Protocol matching must be explained using observed state, not only running-config.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/expectedFindings/1: Source/destination fields and Port operators occur at different steps and therefore cannot replace one another.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/expectedFindings/2: The next step must narrow the hypothesis related to the Extended ACL without disrupting traffic.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/rubric/0/evidence: Clearly identify the device, interface, VLAN/subnet, or policy being observed.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/rubric/0/importance: With the wrong scope, the output may be correct while the conclusion remains incorrect.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Explain the output using at least two concepts from the Extended ACL lesson.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/rubric/1/importance: CCNA assesses reasoning ability, not merely command recognition.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The next step has an expected output and does not change the running configuration.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/rubric/2/importance: Observing before making changes reduces disruption and avoids obscuring the cause.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: First, scope the traffic using the topology and identify Protocol matching as the lesson’s primary decision point.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Then use show ip access-lists POS-POLICY to compare 10 permit tcp 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.50.20.10 eq 443 with 20 permit udp 10.10.10.0 0.0.0.255 host 10.50.1.53 eq 53, thereby separating the actual state from the intended configuration.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Finally, check Port operators at the next hop; propose a change only after new output confirms the cause and a post-change verification method is available.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/commonMistakes/0: Changing the configuration immediately after reading the user’s description.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/commonMistakes/1: Quoting an output line without identifying the device or context to which it belongs.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating a cause without identifying the next output that could confirm or refute it.
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: If traffic flows in the reverse direction, how does the role of Protocol matching change?
ccna-35-extended-acl./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: What additional evidence would you need to explain the Extended ACL lesson to a colleague who has not studied CCNA?
ccna-35-extended-acl./recap/0: Protocol matching: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-35-extended-acl./recap/1: Source/destination fields: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-35-extended-acl./recap/2: Port operators: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-35-extended-acl./recap/3: ACL placement: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-35-extended-acl./selfCheck/0: Can you redraw the data path for the scenario in which POS devices may use only HTTPS to reach the order server and DNS to reach the resolver while all other traffic must be blocked?
ccna-35-extended-acl./selfCheck/1: How would you predict the three important lines of show ip access-lists POS-POLICY?
ccna-35-extended-acl./selfCheck/2: At what level does objective 5.6 require you to explain, configure, verify, or interpret?
ccna-35-extended-acl./selfCheck/3: Which misunderstanding of Protocol matching could lead to changing the wrong device?

ccna-36-cdp-lldp./phaseTitle: Services That Keep the Network Running
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./title: CDP and LLDP: Discovering Neighboring Devices
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./summary: Use Cisco CDP and the open-standard LLDP to verify neighbors, port mappings, platforms, capabilities, and management addresses.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./opening/0: BlueRetail is expanding its retail network, but the cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. Relying on a single command or a single icon in a diagram can easily cause a Junior Network Engineer to make changes in the wrong place. Lesson 36 begins with this incident to answer a practical question: How do CDP and LLDP neighbor discovery affect the data path, and how can the conclusion be proven with output?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./opening/1: Use Cisco CDP and the open-standard LLDP to verify neighbors, port mappings, platforms, capabilities, and management addresses. The goal is not to memorize every table and acronym. You need to explain the data’s journey from the application, through the network interface card, switch, or router, until a response is received. Every decision must be tied to an observable header field, state table, policy, or physical limitation.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./opening/2: The Jeremy’s IT Lab PDF provides the foundational sequence and examples on pages 240–247; the exam scope is aligned with objective 2.3 of Cisco CCNA v1.1. The content has been reworked using the BlueRetail topology and new data, without copying figures or text verbatim from the source material.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/0/title: A Mental Model Before Learning Commands
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of the network as a chain of handoffs supported by evidence. The application creates data, the operating system encapsulates it, the access device places it on the LAN, the router selects a path between networks, and the destination service responds. This CDP and LLDP lesson focuses on one or more handoff points in that chain. If you have not identified the layer at which the data currently resides and which device is allowed to modify it, reading the configuration will quickly become guesswork.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/0/paragraphs/1: When the cabling documentation is outdated and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables, begin by drawing the source, destination, default gateway, and intermediate devices. Then note beside each hop which address or identifier is used, which table is consulted, and what conditions would stop the traffic. This model is small enough to draw on paper but precise enough to explain real output.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/0/paragraphs/2: One sign that you understand Lesson 36 is the ability to predict the state of neighbor discovery before running a command. If the prediction and output differ, do not change the configuration immediately; find the incorrect assumption. This predict-observe-explain process helps distinguish configuration errors, physical faults, protocol issues, and mistakes caused by examining the wrong device.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Neighbor discovery is the first building block in the CDP and LLDP lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, the cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. By connecting neighbor discovery with the local/remote interface, learners can see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/1/paragraphs/1: The local/remote interface is the second building block in the CDP and LLDP lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, the cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. By connecting the local/remote interface with the capability advertisement, learners can see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/1/paragraphs/2: The capability advertisement is the third building block in the CDP and LLDP lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, the cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. By connecting the capability advertisement with the management address, learners can see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The management address is the fourth building block in the CDP and LLDP lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, the cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. By connecting the management address with neighbor discovery, learners can see the cause-and-effect relationship instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/2/title: In What Order Does a Device Process Data?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Trace neighbor discovery across three stages in Lesson 36. First, the device receives a relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 1, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why the same configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting an answer simply because it contains many familiar terms.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Trace the local/remote interface across three stages in Lesson 36. First, the device receives a relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 2, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why the same configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting an answer simply because it contains many familiar terms.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Trace the capability advertisement across three stages in Lesson 36. First, the device receives a relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 3, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why the same configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting an answer simply because it contains many familiar terms.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Trace the management address across three stages in Lesson 36. First, the device receives a relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, discards, logs, or updates a table. At step 4, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why the same configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting an answer simply because it contains many familiar terms.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/3/title: Reading Configuration and CLI Output Without Guessing
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/3/paragraphs/0: The show lldp neighbors detail command is only an observation window for the CDP and LLDP lesson; it does not prove by itself that the entire network is correct. Before reading the output, write down the question to answer—for example, whether the state exists, which interface is involved, which value has priority, and when the output was captured. Then mark each line that supports or disproves the hypothesis.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/3/paragraphs/1: The three sample lines for CDP and LLDP are arranged in this order: identify the object, read the state, and verify the result. If a line is missing, determine whether it should actually appear. Do not treat absence as an error until you understand the entry creation conditions, timers, and the command’s display scope.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When the show lldp neighbors detail output differs from expectations, return to the control plane, data plane, and management plane. A configuration may exist in the running-config while the protocol has not converged; the protocol may be up while a policy still blocks the packet; or the packet may follow the correct path while the management tool reads the wrong VRF or context. Separating these three layers of evidence helps narrow the issue quickly in Lesson 36.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/4/title: Applying the Knowledge to the BlueRetail Network
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/4/paragraphs/0: At BlueRetail, headquarters has two access switches, a pair of distribution devices, an edge router, a firewall, a WLC, service servers, and a connection to the branch. The cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. Learners must first determine the scope of impact: one host, one VLAN, one subnet, one uplink, or the entire site. The clearer the scope, the fewer hypotheses need to be tested.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The next step is to select observation points on both sides of the boundary. For CDP and LLDP, collect one piece of evidence near the source and another near the decision point. If the two sides disagree, check the encapsulation, addressing, timers, policy, or the state of intervening interfaces; do not jump directly to restarting the device.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Finally, for CDP and LLDP, record the conclusion using this structure: observed facts, applicable protocol rule, effect on traffic, and the lowest-risk action. A conclusion that others can verify is better than saying, “The configuration looks correct.” This is also how Lesson 36 knowledge becomes practical operational skill.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/5/title: How to Select the Correct CCNA Answer
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Objective 2.3 of the CDP and LLDP lesson indicates the required level through verbs such as explain, compare, configure, verify, or interpret. If a question asks only to describe, do not infer an out-of-scope configuration; if it asks you to verify, the answer must produce evidence rather than merely repeat the configuration. The objective’s verb determines the required depth of study.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/5/paragraphs/1: When Lesson 36 presents several partially correct answers, first eliminate those that use the wrong layer, traffic direction, or processing order. Then use neighbor-discovery details such as the prefix, metric, port role, adjacency, or policy location to choose the answer. Memory aids are useful only after the processing model is correct.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./sections/5/paragraphs/2: CDP and LLDP are within the official scope; learners must be able to explain both correct behavior and signs of failure. Every answer should end with verifiable output or an observable result.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram showing the relationships among Neighbor discovery, Local/remote interface, Capability advertisement, and Management address in the BlueRetail network.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./cliWalkthroughs/0/context: Condensed output for the scenario in which the cabling documentation is outdated and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/0: The first line identifies the object or scope being examined in the CDP and LLDP lesson.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/1: The second line shows the state calculated by the device, not merely the configuration intent.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/2: The third line provides evidence that connects the state to its effect on BlueRetail traffic.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/3: If the actual output differs from the three sample lines, check the timing, context, interface, and entry creation conditions before changing the configuration.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/0/useWhen: Use the neighbor discovery perspective when the evidence relates to step 1 in the processing sequence.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/0/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines neighbor discovery to explain why the cabling documentation is outdated and how the operations team can identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not equate neighbor discovery with the local/remote interface; they answer two different questions.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/1/useWhen: Use the local/remote interface perspective when the evidence relates to step 2 in the processing sequence.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/1/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the local/remote interface to explain why the cabling documentation is outdated and how the operations team can identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not equate the local/remote interface with the capability advertisement; they answer two different questions.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/2/useWhen: Use the capability advertisement perspective when the evidence relates to step 3 in the processing sequence.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/2/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the capability advertisement to explain why the cabling documentation is outdated and how the operations team can identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not equate the capability advertisement with the management address; they answer two different questions.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the term Neighbor discovery and immediately selecting a command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/0/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to neighbor discovery before making a selection.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 36, neighbor discovery is meaningful only when considered alongside the local/remote interface and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the term Local/remote interface and immediately selecting a command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/1/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the local/remote interface before making a selection.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 36, the local/remote interface is meaningful only when considered alongside the capability advertisement and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the term Capability advertisement and immediately selecting a command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/2/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the capability advertisement before making a selection.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 36, the capability advertisement is meaningful only when considered alongside the management address and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./examFocus/0: Review objective 2.3 and identify the exact required verb.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./examFocus/1: Predict the output of show lldp neighbors detail before viewing the answer.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./examFocus/2: Explain how Neighbor discovery and the Local/remote interface affect the same packet.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./examFocus/3: Recognize common signs of errors without making out-of-scope configuration changes.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/title: Reading the Evidence: CDP and LLDP
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/context: BlueRetail reports that the cabling documentation is outdated, and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables. You are assigned to analyze the available diagram and output before the operations team permits configuration changes.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/learnerRole: You are the Junior Network Engineer responsible for stating a hypothesis, interpreting the evidence, and proposing the next verification step.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/topology: Client → access switch → distribution/router → destination service; the observation path is Neighbor discovery → Local/remote interface → Capability advertisement → Management address.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/evidence/0: The configuration or state related to Neighbor discovery has been provided.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/evidence/1: The show lldp neighbors detail output contains three lines that must be explained in order.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/evidence/2: The user describes the symptom but has not proven that the cause lies with the Local/remote interface.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/questions/0: Which output line provides the strongest evidence, and what does it prove about Neighbor discovery?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/questions/1: Which hypothesis is disproved when the Local/remote interface is compared with the Capability advertisement?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/questions/2: What is the lowest-risk next observation step, and what result would confirm the conclusion?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Redraw the source, destination, Layer 2/Layer 3 boundary, and traffic direction.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Mark where Neighbor discovery is generated, learned, or configured.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Read each line of show lldp neighbors detail and separate facts from assumptions.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Write a conclusion that includes the most likely cause, the impact, and the additional evidence required.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/expectedFindings/0: Neighbor discovery must be explained using observed state, not only the running-config.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/expectedFindings/1: The Local/remote interface and Capability advertisement occur at different steps and therefore cannot replace one another.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/expectedFindings/2: The next step must narrow the hypothesis related to CDP and LLDP without disrupting traffic.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/rubric/0/evidence: Clearly identify the device, interface, VLAN/subnet, or policy being observed.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/rubric/0/importance: If the scope is wrong, the output may be correct while the conclusion remains incorrect.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Explain the output using at least two concepts from the CDP and LLDP lesson.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/rubric/1/importance: CCNA evaluates reasoning ability, not just command recognition.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The next step has an expected output and does not change the running configuration.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/rubric/2/importance: Observing before making changes reduces disruption and avoids obscuring the root cause.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: First, scope the traffic path using the topology and identify Neighbor discovery as the lesson’s primary decision point.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Next, use show lldp neighbors detail to compare Local Intf: Gi1/0/24, Port id: Gi0/1 with System Name: BR-DIST-01, Capabilities: Bridge Router, thereby separating the actual state from the intended configuration.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Finally, check the Capability advertisement at the next hop; propose a change only after new output confirms the cause and a post-change verification method is available.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/commonMistakes/0: Changing the configuration immediately after reading the user’s description.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/commonMistakes/1: Quoting one output line without identifying the device or context to which it belongs.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating a cause without identifying the next output that could confirm or disprove it.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: If traffic flows in the opposite direction, how does the role of Neighbor discovery change?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: What additional evidence would you need to explain the CDP and LLDP lesson to a colleague who has not studied CCNA?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./recap/0: Neighbor discovery: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./recap/1: Local/remote interface: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./recap/2: Capability advertisement: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./recap/3: Management address: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./selfCheck/0: Can you redraw the data path for the scenario in which the cabling documentation is outdated and the operations team needs to identify the actual uplink without unplugging cables?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./selfCheck/1: How would you predict the three important lines of show lldp neighbors detail?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./selfCheck/2: At what level does objective 2.3 require you to explain, configure, verify, or interpret?
ccna-36-cdp-lldp./selfCheck/3: Which misunderstanding about Neighbor discovery could lead to changing the wrong device?
