ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./title: JSON, XML, and YAML: Reading Structured Data
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./summary: Identify objects, arrays, keys/values, and data types, and compare formal JSON with XML/YAML used in automation tools.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./supplementalNote: Supplemental knowledge — not a primary focus of CCNA v1.1
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./opening/0: BlueRetail is expanding its retail system, and its API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. Looking at only one command or one symbol in a diagram can easily lead a Junior Network Engineer to modify the wrong place. Lesson 60 begins with that incident to answer a practical question: how does reading structured data in JSON, XML, and YAML affect the data path, and how can the conclusion be proven with output?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./opening/1: Identify objects, arrays, keys/values, and data types, and compare formal JSON with XML/YAML used in automation tools. The goal is not to memorize every table and acronym. You must be able to describe the data’s journey from the application, through the network interface card, switch, or router, until a response is received. Every decision must be tied to an observable header field, state table, policy, or physical limitation.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./opening/2: The Jeremy’s IT Lab PDF provides the foundational sequence and examples on pages 435–439; the exam scope follows objective 6.7 of Cisco CCNA v1.1. The content is reinterpreted using the BlueRetail topology and new data, without copying figures or text verbatim from the source material.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/0/title: A Mental Model Before Learning Commands
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of a network as a chain of handoffs supported by evidence. The application creates data, the operating system encapsulates it, the access device places it on the LAN, the router selects a path between networks, and the destination service responds. The JSON, XML, and YAML lesson focuses on one or more handoff points in that chain. If you have not identified the layer containing the data and the device authorized to change it, reading the configuration will quickly become guesswork.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/0/paragraphs/1: When an API returns JSON but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element, begin by drawing the source, destination, default gateway, and intermediate devices. Beside each hop, note which address or identifier is used, which table is consulted, and which conditions cause traffic to stop. This model is small enough to draw on paper but precise enough to explain real output.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/0/paragraphs/2: One sign that you understand lesson 60 is that you can predict the state of a JSON object/array before running a command. If the prediction and output differ, do not immediately change the configuration; find the incorrect assumption. This predict-observe-explain process helps distinguish configuration errors, physical faults, protocol failures, and errors caused by examining the wrong device.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/1/paragraphs/0: JSON object/array is the first building block of the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, an API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. By connecting JSON object/array with key and value types, learners can see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/1/paragraphs/1: Key and value types is the second building block of the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, an API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. By connecting key and value types with the XML element tree, learners can see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/1/paragraphs/2: XML element tree is the third building block of the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, an API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. By connecting the XML element tree with YAML indentation, learners can see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/1/paragraphs/3: YAML indentation is the fourth building block of the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, an API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. By connecting YAML indentation with JSON object/array, learners can see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely remembering command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device shows that the change has actually taken effect.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/2/title: In What Order Does a Device Process Data?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Track JSON object/array across three points in lesson 60. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 1, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Track key and value types across three points in lesson 60. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 2, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Track the XML element tree across three points in lesson 60. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 3, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Track YAML indentation across three points in lesson 60. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 4, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration may appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/3/title: Read Configuration and CLI Output Without Guessing
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/3/paragraphs/0: The python -m json.tool response.json command is only an observation window for the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson; it does not prove by itself that the entire network is operating correctly. Before reading the output, write down the question to be answered, such as whether the state exists, which interface is involved, which value takes precedence, and when the output was captured. Then mark each line that supports or refutes the hypothesis.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/3/paragraphs/1: The three sample lines for JSON, XML, and YAML are arranged in this order: identify the object, read the state, and verify the result. If a line is missing, explain whether it should actually appear. Do not treat absence as an error until you understand the conditions that create the entry, its timer, and the command’s display scope.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When the output of python -m json.tool response.json differs from expectations, return to the control plane, data plane, and management plane. The configuration may exist in the running-config while the protocol has not converged; the protocol may be up while a policy still blocks the packet; or the packet may follow the correct path while the management tool reads the wrong VRF or context. Separating these three layers of evidence helps narrow the issue quickly in lesson 60.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/4/title: Apply the Knowledge to the BlueRetail Network
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/4/paragraphs/0: At BlueRetail, headquarters has two access switches, a pair of distribution devices, an edge router, a firewall, a WLC, service servers, and branch connectivity. The API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. Learners must first determine the scope of impact: one host, one VLAN, one subnet, one uplink path, or the entire site. The clearer the scope, the fewer hypotheses must be tested.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The next step is to select observation points on both sides of the boundary. For JSON, XML, and YAML, collect one piece of evidence near the source and another near the decision point. If the two sides disagree, inspect the encapsulation, addresses, timers, policies, or interface state between them; do not jump directly to restarting a device.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Finally, for JSON, XML, and YAML, record the conclusion using this structure: observed facts, applicable protocol rule, effect on traffic, and lowest-risk action. A conclusion that others can verify is better than saying, “the configuration looks correct.” This is also how knowledge from lesson 60 becomes practical operational skill.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/5/title: How to Select the Correct CCNA Answer
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Objective 6.7 of the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson indicates the required level through verbs such as explain, compare, configure, verify, or interpret. If the question asks only to describe, do not infer an out-of-scope configuration; if it asks you to verify, the answer must produce evidence rather than merely repeat the configuration. The objective’s verb determines the required depth of study.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/5/paragraphs/1: When lesson 60 presents multiple partially correct answers, first eliminate those with the wrong layer, traffic direction, or processing order. Then use details related to JSON object/array, such as the prefix, metric, port role, adjacency, or policy location, to choose. Memory aids are useful only after the processing model is correct.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./sections/5/paragraphs/2: This is supplemental knowledge, not a primary focus of CCNA v1.1 in the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson. The content is retained for comparison and for reading legacy systems, but the self-check does not assume that it is a core configuration requirement. Every answer should end with verifiable output or an observable result.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./visuals/0/title: Concept Map: JSON, XML, and YAML
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram showing the relationships among JSON object/array, Key and value types, XML element tree, and YAML indentation in the BlueRetail network.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./visuals/0/connections/0: JSON object/array → Key and value types
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./visuals/0/connections/1: Key and value types → XML element tree
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./cliWalkthroughs/0/context: Abbreviated output for the scenario in which an API returns JSON but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/0: The first line identifies the object or scope being examined in the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/1: The second line shows the state calculated by the device, not merely the intended configuration.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/2: The third line provides evidence linking the state to its effect on BlueRetail traffic.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/3: If the actual output differs from the three sample lines, check the time, context, interface, and entry-creation conditions before changing the configuration.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/0/useWhen: Use the JSON object/array perspective when the evidence relates to step 1 in the processing sequence.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/0/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines JSON object/array to explain why the API returns JSON but the engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not treat JSON object/array and key and value types as equivalent; they answer two different questions.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/1/useWhen: Use the key and value types perspective when the evidence relates to step 2 in the processing sequence.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/1/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines key and value types to explain why the API returns JSON but the engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not treat key and value types and the XML element tree as equivalent; they answer two different questions.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/2/useWhen: Use the XML element tree perspective when the evidence relates to step 3 in the processing sequence.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/2/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the XML element tree to explain why the API returns JSON but the engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not treat the XML element tree and YAML indentation as equivalent; they answer two different questions.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the term JSON object/array and immediately selecting the command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/0/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to JSON object/array before making a selection.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/0/reason: In lesson 60, JSON object/array is meaningful only when considered alongside key and value types and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the term Key and value types and immediately selecting the command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/1/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to key and value types before making a selection.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/1/reason: In lesson 60, key and value types is meaningful only when considered alongside the XML element tree and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the term XML element tree and immediately selecting the command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/2/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the XML element tree before making a selection.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./commonMistakes/2/reason: In lesson 60, the XML element tree is meaningful only when considered alongside YAML indentation and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./examFocus/0: Review objective 6.7 and identify the exact required verb.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./examFocus/1: Predict the output of python -m json.tool response.json before viewing the answer.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./examFocus/2: Explain how JSON object/array and Key and value types affect the same packet.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./examFocus/3: Recognize common signs of an error without making out-of-scope configuration changes.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/title: Reading the Evidence: JSON, XML, and YAML
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/context: BlueRetail reports that the API returns JSON, but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element. You are assigned to analyze the provided diagram and output before the operations team permits configuration changes.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/learnerRole: You are the Junior Network Engineer responsible for stating a hypothesis, reading the evidence, and proposing the next verification step.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/topology: Client → access switch → distribution/router → destination service; the observation sequence is JSON object/array → Key and value types → XML element tree → YAML indentation.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/evidence/0: The configuration or state related to JSON object/array has been provided.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/evidence/1: The output of python -m json.tool response.json contains three lines that must be explained in order.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/evidence/2: The user describes the symptom but has not proven that Key and value types is the cause.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/questions/0: Which output line provides the strongest evidence, and what does it prove about JSON object/array?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/questions/1: Which hypothesis is refuted when Key and value types is compared with the XML element tree?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/questions/2: What is the lowest-risk next observation step, and what result would confirm the conclusion?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Redraw the source, destination, Layer 2/Layer 3 boundaries, and traffic direction.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Mark where JSON object/array is created, learned, or configured.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Read each line of python -m json.tool response.json and separate facts from assumptions.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Write a conclusion that includes the most likely cause, its impact, and the additional evidence required.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/expectedFindings/0: JSON object/array must be explained using observed state, not only the running-config.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/expectedFindings/1: Key and value types and the XML element tree occur at different steps and cannot replace each other.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/expectedFindings/2: The next step must narrow the hypothesis related to JSON, XML, and YAML without disrupting traffic.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/rubric/0/evidence: Clearly identify the device, interface, VLAN/subnet, or policy being observed.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/rubric/0/importance: An incorrect scope can make the output correct while the conclusion remains wrong.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Explain the output using at least two concepts from the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/rubric/1/importance: CCNA assesses reasoning ability, not merely command recognition.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The next step has an expected output and does not change the running configuration.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/rubric/2/importance: Observing before making changes reduces disruption and avoids obscuring the root cause.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: First, scope the traffic using the topology and identify JSON object/array as the lesson’s primary decision point.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Then use python -m json.tool response.json to compare {"interfaces": [ with   {"name": "GigabitEthernet1", "enabled": true}, thereby separating the actual state from the intended configuration.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Finally, inspect the XML element tree at the next hop; propose a change only after new output confirms the cause and a post-change verification method is available.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/commonMistakes/0: Changing the configuration immediately after reading the user’s description.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/commonMistakes/1: Quoting one output line without identifying the device or context to which it belongs.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating a cause without identifying the next output that could confirm or refute it.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: If traffic flows in the opposite direction, how does the role of JSON object/array change?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: What additional evidence would you need to explain the JSON, XML, and YAML lesson to a colleague who has not studied CCNA?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./recap/0: JSON object/array: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./recap/1: Key and value types: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./recap/2: XML element tree: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./recap/3: YAML indentation: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./selfCheck/0: Can you redraw the data path for the scenario in which an API returns JSON but an engineer treats the interface list as a single object and misses an element?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./selfCheck/1: How would you predict the three important lines from python -m json.tool response.json?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./selfCheck/2: At what level does objective 6.7 require you to explain, configure, verify, or interpret?
ccna-60-json-xml-yaml./selfCheck/3: What misunderstanding about JSON object/array could lead to changing the wrong device?

ccna-61-rest-api./title: REST API, CRUD, HTTP Verbs, and Authentication
ccna-61-rest-api./summary: Interpret resource URIs, GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE, status codes, JSON payloads, and API authentication types.
ccna-61-rest-api./opening/0: BlueRetail is expanding its retail system, and a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. Looking at only one command or one diagram icon can easily lead a Junior Network Engineer to modify the wrong place. Lesson 61 begins with this incident to answer a practical question: How do REST APIs, CRUD, HTTP verbs, and authentication affect the data path, and how can the conclusion be verified through output?
ccna-61-rest-api./opening/1: Interpret resource URIs, GET/POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE, status codes, JSON payloads, and API authentication types. The goal is not to memorize every table and acronym. You need to describe the data journey from the application, through the network interface card, switch, or router, until a response is received. Every decision must be tied to an observable header field, state table, policy, or physical limitation.
ccna-61-rest-api./opening/2: The Jeremy’s IT Lab PDF provides the foundational sequence and examples on pages 440–443; the exam scope is defined by objective 6.5 of Cisco CCNA v1.1. CCNA v1.1 adds authentication types for REST APIs alongside CRUD, HTTP verbs, and data encoding.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/0/title: Build a Mental Model Before Learning Commands
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of the network as a chain of evidence-based handoffs. The application creates data, the operating system encapsulates it, the access device places it on the LAN, the router selects a path between networks, and the destination service responds. This REST API lesson focuses on one or more handoff points in that chain. Unless you identify the layer containing the data and the device authorized to modify it, reading the configuration quickly becomes guesswork.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/0/paragraphs/1: When a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct, begin by drawing the source, destination, default gateway, and intermediate devices. Beside each hop, note which address or identifier is used, which table is consulted, and which conditions stop the traffic. This model is small enough to draw on paper but precise enough to explain actual output.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/0/paragraphs/2: One sign that you understand lesson 61 is the ability to predict the state of the resource and URI before running a command. If the prediction differs from the output, do not change the configuration immediately; find the incorrect assumption. This predict-observe-explain process helps distinguish configuration errors, physical faults, protocol errors, and errors caused by examining the wrong device.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/1/paragraphs/0: Resource and URI are the first component of the REST API lesson. Do not learn them as isolated definitions. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. Connecting resource and URI to HTTP methods/CRUD allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely memorizing command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change actually took effect.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/1/paragraphs/1: HTTP methods/CRUD are the second component of the REST API lesson. Do not learn them as isolated definitions. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. Connecting HTTP methods/CRUD to status codes allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely memorizing command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change actually took effect.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/1/paragraphs/2: Status codes are the third component of the REST API lesson. Do not learn them as isolated definitions. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. Connecting status codes to API authentication allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely memorizing command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change actually took effect.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/1/paragraphs/3: API authentication is the fourth component of the REST API lesson. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which field it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. Connecting API authentication to resource and URI allows learners to see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely memorizing command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device proves that the change actually took effect.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/2/title: In What Order Does a Device Process Data?
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Track resource and URI at three points in lesson 61. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data against its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated explicitly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 1, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. On CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Track HTTP methods/CRUD at three points in lesson 61. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data against its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated explicitly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 2, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. On CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Track status codes at three points in lesson 61. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data against its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated explicitly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 3, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. On CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Track API authentication at three points in lesson 61. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data against its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated explicitly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 4, BlueRetail must distinguish what the device “knows” from what the engineer “expects.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output remains incorrect. On CCNA questions, follow the actual processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terms.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/3/title: Read Configuration and CLI Output Without Guessing
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/3/paragraphs/0: The curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces command is only an observation window for the REST API lesson; it does not prove that the entire network is functioning correctly. Before reading its output, write down the question to answer, such as whether the state exists, which interface is involved, which value has priority, and when the output was captured. Then mark each line that supports or refutes the hypothesis.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/3/paragraphs/1: The three sample REST API lines are ordered to identify the object, read its state, and verify the result. If a line is missing, determine whether it should actually appear. Do not treat absence as an error until you understand the conditions that create the entry, its timer, and the command's display scope.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When the output of curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces differs from expectations, return to the control plane, data plane, and management plane. The configuration may exist in running-config while the protocol has not converged; the protocol may be up while a policy still blocks the packet; or the packet may follow the correct path while the management tool reads the wrong VRF or context. Separating these three layers of evidence helps narrow the issue quickly in lesson 61.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/4/title: Apply the Concepts to the BlueRetail Network
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/4/paragraphs/0: At BlueRetail, headquarters has two access switches, a pair of distribution devices, an edge router, a firewall, a WLC, service servers, and branch connectivity. A script receives a 401 Unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. Learners must first determine the scope of impact: one host, one VLAN, one subnet, one uplink, or the entire site. The clearer the scope, the fewer hypotheses need to be tested.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The next step is to select observation points on both sides of the boundary. For a REST API, collect one piece of evidence near the source and another near the decision point. If the evidence from both sides is inconsistent, check the encapsulation, addresses, timers, policies, or interface state between them; do not jump directly to restarting the device.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Finally, for a REST API, record the conclusion using this structure: observed facts, applicable protocol rule, effect on traffic, and the lowest-risk action. A conclusion that others can verify is better than saying, “The configuration looks correct.” This is also how lesson 61 knowledge becomes practical operational skill.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/5/title: How to Select the Correct CCNA Answer
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Objective 6.5 for the REST API lesson indicates the required level through verbs such as explain, compare, configure, verify, or interpret. If the question asks only to describe, do not infer an out-of-scope configuration. If it asks you to verify, the answer must produce evidence rather than merely repeat the configuration. The objective's verb determines the required depth of study.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/5/paragraphs/1: When lesson 61 offers several partially correct answers, first eliminate those with the wrong layer, traffic direction, or processing order. Then use details related to resource and URI, such as prefix, metric, port role, adjacency, or policy location, to choose the answer. Memory aids are useful only after the processing model is correct.
ccna-61-rest-api./sections/5/paragraphs/2: The REST API lesson is within the official scope; learners must be able to explain both correct behavior and signs of failure. Every answer should end with verifiable output or an observable result.
ccna-61-rest-api./visuals/0/caption: The original diagram shows the relationships among Resource and URI, HTTP methods/CRUD, Status codes, and API authentication in the BlueRetail network.
ccna-61-rest-api./cliWalkthroughs/0/context: Condensed output for the scenario in which a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct.
ccna-61-rest-api./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/0: The first line identifies the object or scope being examined in the REST API lesson.
ccna-61-rest-api./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/1: The second line shows the state calculated by the device, not merely the intended configuration.
ccna-61-rest-api./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/2: The third line provides evidence connecting the state to its effect on BlueRetail traffic.
ccna-61-rest-api./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/3: If the actual output differs from the three sample lines, check the timing, context, interface, and entry-creation conditions before changing the configuration.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/0/useWhen: Use the resource and URI perspective when the evidence relates to step 1 of the processing sequence.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/0/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the resource and URI to explain why a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not equate resource and URI with HTTP methods/CRUD; they answer different questions.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/1/useWhen: Use the HTTP methods/CRUD perspective when the evidence relates to step 2 of the processing sequence.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/1/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines HTTP methods/CRUD to explain why a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not equate HTTP methods/CRUD with status codes; they answer different questions.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/2/useWhen: Use the status-code perspective when the evidence relates to step 3 of the processing sequence.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/2/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines status codes to explain why a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct.
ccna-61-rest-api./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not equate status codes with API authentication; they answer different questions.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the term Resource and URI and immediately selecting a command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/0/better: Before choosing, identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the resource and URI.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/0/reason: In lesson 61, resource and URI are meaningful only when considered alongside HTTP methods/CRUD and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the term HTTP methods/CRUD and immediately selecting a command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/1/better: Before choosing, identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to HTTP methods/CRUD.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/1/reason: In lesson 61, HTTP methods/CRUD are meaningful only when considered alongside status codes and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the term Status codes and immediately selecting a command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/2/better: Before choosing, identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to status codes.
ccna-61-rest-api./commonMistakes/2/reason: In lesson 61, status codes are meaningful only when considered alongside API authentication and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-61-rest-api./examFocus/0: Review objective 6.5 and identify the required verb correctly.
ccna-61-rest-api./examFocus/1: Predict the output of curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces before viewing the answer.
ccna-61-rest-api./examFocus/2: Explain how Resource and URI and HTTP methods/CRUD affect the same packet.
ccna-61-rest-api./examFocus/3: Recognize common signs of failure without making out-of-scope configuration changes.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/context: BlueRetail reports that a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct. You are assigned to analyze the available diagram and output before the operations team permits configuration changes.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/learnerRole: You are the Junior Network Engineer responsible for stating a hypothesis, interpreting the evidence, and recommending the next diagnostic step.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/topology: Client → access switch → distribution/router → destination service; the observation focus is Resource and URI → HTTP methods/CRUD → Status codes → API authentication.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/evidence/0: The configuration or state related to Resource and URI has been provided.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/evidence/1: The output of curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces contains three lines that must be explained in order.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/evidence/2: The user describes the symptom but has not proved that the cause lies in HTTP methods/CRUD.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/questions/0: Which output line provides the strongest evidence, and what does it prove about Resource and URI?
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/questions/1: Which hypothesis is disproved by comparing HTTP methods/CRUD with Status codes?
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/questions/2: What is the lowest-risk next observation step, and what result would confirm the conclusion?
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Redraw the source, destination, Layer 2/Layer 3 boundary, and traffic direction.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Mark where Resource and URI are created, learned, or configured.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Read each line from curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces, separating facts from assumptions.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Write a conclusion containing the most likely cause, its impact, and the additional evidence required.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/expectedFindings/0: Resource and URI must be explained using observed state, not only running-config.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/expectedFindings/1: HTTP methods/CRUD and Status codes occur at different steps and therefore cannot replace one another.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/expectedFindings/2: The next step must narrow the REST API-related hypotheses without disrupting traffic.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/rubric/0/evidence: Specify the device, interface, VLAN/subnet, or policy being observed.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/rubric/0/importance: With the wrong scope, the output may be correct while the conclusion remains wrong.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Explain the output using at least two concepts from the REST API lesson.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/rubric/1/importance: CCNA assesses reasoning ability, not merely command recognition.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The next step has an expected output and does not change the running configuration.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/rubric/2/importance: Observing before making changes reduces disruption and avoids obscuring the root cause.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: First, define the traffic scope using the topology and identify Resource and URI as the lesson's primary decision point.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Next, use curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces to compare HTTP/2 200 OK with content-type: application/json, separating the actual state from the intended configuration.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Finally, check Status codes at the next hop; recommend a change only after new output confirms the cause and a post-change verification method is available.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/commonMistakes/0: Changing the configuration immediately after reading the user's description.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/commonMistakes/1: Quoting one output line without identifying the device or context to which it belongs.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating a cause without identifying the next output that could confirm or refute it.
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: If traffic flows in the opposite direction, how does the role of Resource and URI change?
ccna-61-rest-api./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: What additional evidence would you need to explain the REST API lesson to a colleague who has not studied CCNA?
ccna-61-rest-api./recap/0: Resource and URI: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-61-rest-api./recap/1: HTTP methods/CRUD: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-61-rest-api./recap/2: Status codes: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-61-rest-api./recap/3: API authentication: identify the input, processing rule, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-61-rest-api./selfCheck/0: Can you redraw the data path for the scenario in which a script receives a 401 unauthorized response even though the endpoint and method are correct?
ccna-61-rest-api./selfCheck/1: How would you predict the three important lines from curl -i -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://controller/api/interfaces?
ccna-61-rest-api./selfCheck/2: At what level does objective 6.5 require you to explain, configure, verify, or interpret?
ccna-61-rest-api./selfCheck/3: Which misunderstanding about Resource and URI could lead to modifying the wrong device?

ccna-62-sdn./summary: Distinguish between the control and data planes, northbound and southbound APIs, overlay, underlay, and fabric architectures, and controller-based networking.
ccna-62-sdn./opening/0: BlueRetail is expanding its retail system, and the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI. Looking at only one command or one icon in a diagram can easily lead a Junior Network Engineer to make changes in the wrong place. Lesson 62 begins with that incident to answer a practical question: How do software-defined networking, controllers, and intent affect the path of data, and how can the conclusion be validated with output?
ccna-62-sdn./opening/1: Distinguish between the control and data planes, northbound and southbound APIs, overlay, underlay, and fabric architectures, and controller-based networking. The goal is not to memorize every table and acronym. You need to be able to describe the journey of data from the application, through the network interface card, switch, or router, until a response is received. Every decision must be tied to an observable header field, state table, policy, or physical constraint.
ccna-62-sdn./opening/2: The Jeremy’s IT Lab PDF provides the foundational sequence and examples on pages 444–449; the exam scope is aligned with objectives 6.1, 6.2, and 6.3 of Cisco CCNA v1.1. The content has been reworked using the BlueRetail topology and new data, without copying figures or text verbatim from the source material.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/0/title: Build a Mental Model Before Learning Commands
ccna-62-sdn./sections/0/paragraphs/0: Think of a network as a chain of handoffs supported by evidence. The application creates data, the operating system encapsulates it, the access device introduces it into the LAN, the router selects a path between networks, and the destination service responds. The lesson on SDN and controllers focuses on one or more handoff points in this chain. If you have not identified the layer at which the data is operating and which device is authorized to modify it, reading the configuration will quickly become guesswork.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/0/paragraphs/1: When the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI, begin by drawing the source, destination, default gateway, and intermediate devices. Then note beside each hop which address or identifier is used, which table is consulted, and which conditions cause traffic to stop. This model is small enough to draw on paper but precise enough to explain actual output.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/0/paragraphs/2: One sign that you understand Lesson 62 is that you can predict the state of the control and data planes before running a command. If the prediction differs from the output, do not immediately change the configuration; identify the incorrect assumption. The predict-observe-explain process helps distinguish configuration errors, physical faults, protocol failures, and errors caused by inspecting the wrong device.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/1/paragraphs/0: The control/data plane is the first building block in the lesson on SDN and controllers. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which fields it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI. By connecting the control/data plane to the central controller, learners can see cause-and-effect relationships instead of merely memorizing command names. A good explanation must identify the state before processing, where the change occurs, and what evidence on the device demonstrates that the change has actually taken effect.

ccna-62-sdn./sections/1/paragraphs/3: The underlay/overlay fabric is the fourth building block in the lesson on SDN and controllers. Do not learn it as an isolated definition. Ask what input the device or protocol receives, which fields it uses to make a decision, and what result it produces for the next step. In the BlueRetail network, the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI. ccna-62-sdn./sections/2/title: In What Order Does a Device Process Data?
ccna-62-sdn./sections/2/paragraphs/0: Track the control/data plane at three points in Lesson 62. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 1, BlueRetail must distinguish between what the device “knows” and what the engineer “intends.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the exact processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terminology.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/2/paragraphs/1: Track the central controller at three points in Lesson 62. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 2, BlueRetail must distinguish between what the device “knows” and what the engineer “intends.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the exact processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terminology.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/2/paragraphs/2: Track northbound/southbound APIs at three points in Lesson 62. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 3, BlueRetail must distinguish between what the device “knows” and what the engineer “intends.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. When answering CCNA questions, follow the exact processing order instead of selecting the answer containing the most familiar terminology.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/2/paragraphs/3: Track the underlay/overlay fabric at three points in Lesson 62. First, the device receives the relevant frame, packet, control information, or configuration. Next, it compares that data with its current state tables and rules; if information is missing, the default behavior must be stated clearly. Finally, it forwards, drops, logs, or updates a table. At step 4, BlueRetail must distinguish between what the device “knows” and what the engineer “intends.” This gap explains why a configuration can appear reasonable while the operational output is still incorrect. ccna-62-sdn./sections/3/title: Read Configuration and CLI Output Without Guessing
ccna-62-sdn./sections/3/paragraphs/0: The curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count command is only an observation window for the lesson on SDN and controllers; it does not prove by itself that the entire network is operating correctly. Before reading the output, write down the question to be answered, such as whether the state exists, which interface is involved, which value takes precedence, and when the output was captured. Then mark each line that supports or refutes the hypothesis.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/3/paragraphs/1: The three sample lines for SDN and controllers are arranged in this order: identify the object, read the state, and verify the result. If a line is missing, explain whether it should actually be present. Do not treat absence as an error until you understand the conditions that create the entry, its timer, and the command's display scope.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/3/paragraphs/2: When the output of curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count differs from expectations, return to the control plane, data plane, and management plane. The configuration may exist in the running-config while the protocol has not yet converged; the protocol may be up while a policy still blocks the packet; or the packet may follow the correct path while the management tool reads the wrong VRF or context. Separating these three evidence layers helps narrow the issue quickly in Lesson 62.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/4/title: Apply the Knowledge to the BlueRetail Network
ccna-62-sdn./sections/4/paragraphs/0: At BlueRetail, headquarters has two access switches, a pair of distribution devices, an edge router, a firewall, a WLC, service servers, and connectivity to branch offices. The network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI. Learners must first determine the scope of impact: one host, one VLAN, one subnet, one uplink path, or the entire site. The clearer the scope, the fewer hypotheses need to be tested.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/4/paragraphs/1: The next step is to select observation points on both sides of the boundary. For SDN and controllers, collect one piece of evidence near the source and another near the decision point. If the two sides do not agree, check the encapsulation, addresses, timers, policies, or interface states between them; do not jump directly to restarting the device.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/4/paragraphs/2: Finally, for SDN and controllers, document the conclusion using this format: observed facts, applicable protocol rule, impact on traffic, and the lowest-risk action. A conclusion that others can verify is better than saying, “The configuration looks correct.” This is also how knowledge from Lesson 62 becomes practical operational capability.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/5/title: How to Select the Correct CCNA Answer
ccna-62-sdn./sections/5/paragraphs/0: Objectives 6.1, 6.2, and 6.3 of the lesson on SDN and controllers specify required skills such as explain, compare, configure, verify, or interpret. If the question asks only for a description, do not infer an out-of-scope configuration; if it asks you to verify, the answer must produce evidence rather than merely restate the configuration. The objective's verb identifies the required depth of study.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/5/paragraphs/1: When Lesson 62 includes several partially correct answers, first eliminate answers that use the wrong layer, traffic direction, or processing order. Then use details related to the control/data plane, such as a prefix, metric, port role, adjacency, or policy location, to choose the answer. Mnemonics are useful only after the processing model is correct.
ccna-62-sdn./sections/5/paragraphs/2: The lesson on SDN and controllers is within the official scope; learners must be able to explain both correct behavior and signs of failure. Every answer should end with verifiable output or an observable result.
ccna-62-sdn./visuals/0/caption: An original diagram showing the relationships among the Control/data plane, Central controller, Northbound/southbound API, and Underlay/overlay fabric in the BlueRetail network.
ccna-62-sdn./cliWalkthroughs/0/context: Abbreviated output for a scenario in which the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI.
ccna-62-sdn./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/0: The first line identifies the object or scope being examined in the lesson on SDN and controllers.
ccna-62-sdn./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/1: The second line shows the state calculated by the device, not merely the intended configuration.
ccna-62-sdn./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/2: The third line provides evidence connecting the state to its impact on BlueRetail traffic.
ccna-62-sdn./cliWalkthroughs/0/annotations/3: If the actual output differs from the three sample lines, check the timing, context, interface, and entry-creation conditions before changing the configuration.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/0/useWhen: Use the control/data plane perspective when the evidence relates to step 1 in the processing sequence.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/0/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the control/data plane to explain why the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/0/watchOut: Do not equate the control/data plane with the central controller; they answer two different questions.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/1/useWhen: Use the central controller perspective when the evidence relates to step 2 in the processing sequence.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/1/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines the central controller to explain why the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/1/watchOut: Do not equate the central controller with northbound/southbound APIs; they answer two different questions.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/2/useWhen: Use the northbound/southbound API perspective when the evidence relates to step 3 in the processing sequence.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/2/blueRetailExample: BlueRetail examines northbound/southbound APIs to explain why the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI.
ccna-62-sdn./comparison/2/watchOut: Do not equate northbound/southbound APIs with the underlay/overlay fabric; they answer two different questions.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/0/wrong: Seeing the term Control/data plane and immediately selecting the command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/0/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the control/data plane before selecting an answer.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/0/reason: In Lesson 62, the control/data plane is meaningful only when considered alongside the central controller and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/1/wrong: Seeing the term Central controller and immediately selecting the command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/1/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to the central controller before selecting an answer.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/1/reason: In Lesson 62, the central controller is meaningful only when considered alongside northbound/southbound APIs and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/2/wrong: Seeing the term Northbound/southbound API and immediately selecting the command or answer containing the same term.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/2/better: Identify the layer, traffic direction, state, and evidence related to northbound/southbound APIs before selecting an answer.
ccna-62-sdn./commonMistakes/2/reason: In Lesson 62, northbound/southbound APIs are meaningful only when considered alongside the underlay/overlay fabric and the BlueRetail topology.
ccna-62-sdn./examFocus/0: Review objectives 6.1, 6.2, and 6.3, and identify the required verb correctly.
ccna-62-sdn./examFocus/1: Predict the output of curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count before viewing the answer.
ccna-62-sdn./examFocus/2: Explain how the Control/data plane and Central controller affect the same packet.
ccna-62-sdn./examFocus/3: Recognize common signs of an error without making out-of-scope configuration changes.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/title: Reading the Evidence: SDN and Controllers
ccna-62-sdn./activity/context: BlueRetail reports that the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI. You are assigned to analyze the existing diagram and output before the operations team permits configuration changes.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/learnerRole: You are the Junior Network Engineer responsible for stating a hypothesis, examining the evidence, and proposing the next verification step.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/topology: Client → access switch → distribution/router → destination service; the observation path focuses on Control/data plane → Central controller → Northbound/southbound API → Underlay/overlay fabric.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/evidence/0: The configuration or state related to the Control/data plane has been provided.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/evidence/1: The output of curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count contains three lines that must be explained in sequence.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/evidence/2: The user has described the symptom but has not demonstrated that the cause lies in the Central controller.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/questions/0: Which output line is the strongest evidence, and what does it demonstrate about the Control/data plane?
ccna-62-sdn./activity/questions/1: Which hypothesis is disproved by comparing the Central controller with the Northbound/southbound API?
ccna-62-sdn./activity/questions/2: What is the lowest-risk next observation step, and what result would confirm the conclusion?
ccna-62-sdn./activity/reasoningSteps/0: Redraw the source, destination, Layer 2/Layer 3 boundaries, and traffic direction.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/reasoningSteps/1: Mark where the Control/data plane is created, learned, or configured.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/reasoningSteps/2: Read each line of curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count, separating facts from assumptions.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/reasoningSteps/3: Write a conclusion that includes the most likely cause, its impact, and the additional evidence required.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/expectedFindings/0: The Control/data plane must be explained using observed state, not only the running-config.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/expectedFindings/1: The Central controller and Northbound/southbound API operate at different steps and therefore cannot replace each other.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/expectedFindings/2: The next step must narrow the hypothesis related to SDN and controllers without disrupting traffic.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/rubric/0/evidence: Clearly identify the device, interface, VLAN/subnet, or policy being observed.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/rubric/0/importance: If the scope is wrong, the output may be correct while the conclusion remains incorrect.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/rubric/1/evidence: Explain the output using at least two concepts from the lesson on SDN and controllers.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/rubric/1/importance: CCNA assesses reasoning ability, not merely command recognition.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/rubric/2/evidence: The next step has an expected output and does not modify the running configuration.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/rubric/2/importance: Observing before making changes reduces disruption and avoids obscuring the cause.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/sampleAnalysis/0: First, scope the traffic using the topology and identify the Control/data plane as the lesson's primary decision point.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/sampleAnalysis/1: Then use curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count to compare {"response": 128, with  "version": "1.0"}, thereby separating the actual state from the intended configuration.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/sampleAnalysis/2: Finally, examine the Northbound/southbound API at the next hop; propose a change only after new output confirms the cause and a post-change verification method is available.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/commonMistakes/0: Changing the configuration immediately after reading the user's description.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/commonMistakes/1: Quoting one line of output without identifying the device or context to which it belongs.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/commonMistakes/2: Stating a cause without identifying the next output that could confirm or refute it.
ccna-62-sdn./activity/reflectionQuestions/0: If traffic flows in the opposite direction, how does the role of the Control/data plane change?
ccna-62-sdn./activity/reflectionQuestions/1: What additional evidence would you need to explain the lesson on SDN and controllers to a colleague who has not studied CCNA?
ccna-62-sdn./recap/0: Control/data plane: Identify the input, processing rules, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-62-sdn./recap/1: Central controller: Identify the input, processing rules, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-62-sdn./recap/2: Northbound/southbound API: Identify the input, processing rules, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-62-sdn./recap/3: Underlay/overlay fabric: Identify the input, processing rules, resulting state, and output used for verification.
ccna-62-sdn./selfCheck/0: Can you redraw the data path for the scenario in which the network team wants to apply the same segmentation policy across multiple devices without manually configuring each CLI?
ccna-62-sdn./selfCheck/1: How would you predict the three important lines of output from curl -s https://controller/api/v1/network-device/count?
ccna-62-sdn./selfCheck/2: At what depth do objectives 6.1, 6.2, and 6.3 require you to explain, configure, verify, or interpret?
ccna-62-sdn./selfCheck/3: Which misunderstanding about the Control/data plane could lead to changing the wrong device?
